note: Curated, user-facing release notes for the prod (main + demo) track. Keyed by the CH12 prod version. Add a new entry at the top of `releases` each time the prod version bumps (see chore(release) boundaries on main). Keep it readable — this is for users, not a git log.

releases.title: Dashboard tabs, a friendlier dashboard, and mobile fixes
releases.summary: Named dashboard tabs graduate from staging — now with drag-to-reorder, a close button, and an explicit Save for layout changes. The dashboard gains a visible 'Add modules' picker and a live activity log, the calendar finally behaves on phones, and a rotating bit of desk wisdom lives in the header. Your trade data still never leaves the browser.
highlights: Dashboard tabs on every surface: create, rename, close (✕), and drag tabs to reorder; each tab keeps its own module layout. Layout edits now stage with an unsaved-changes asterisk until you hit Save.
'+ Add modules' is always visible on the dashboard and opens an illustrated picker — check the modules you want and add them in one go.
The Activity log now works: it backfills the session's boot events (session initiated, data loaded) and appends live actions like CSV imports, saved filters, and new tabs.
Mobile: calendars fit the screen with square, bounded day cells (no more clipped P&L), no screen scrolls sideways anymore, and long data-feed names truncate instead of breaking the layout.

Polish: saved filters are now called saved filters, the sidebar shows the real Blotterbook mark, the import preview warns about skipped fills and still-open positions, and the privacy page now describes exactly which requests the app makes.

releases.title: More accurate numbers everywhere — plus tag analytics and a faster app
releases.summary: A top-to-bottom audit of every calculation Blotterbook performs, with fixes across CSV import, drawdown and profit-factor edge cases, commission tiers, and report labeling. Also new: per-tag performance analytics, day-tag surfacing with autocomplete, and a lighter first load. Your trade data still never leaves the browser.
highlights: CSV import is tougher: semicolon-delimited files (EU Excel re-saves) parse correctly, ambiguous dates can no longer be flipped by a stray note, IBKR timestamps keep their time of day, and unreadable fills are skipped with a visible notice instead of corrupting the import.

Edge-case metrics tell the truth: drawdowns that start at your first trade show '—' instead of a misleading 0.0% of peak, tiny rounding residue can no longer produce absurd profit-concentration percentages or a red -$0.00, and $0 scratch trades are footnoted rather than counted as wins.

Commission estimates cover 33 futures roots (treasuries, grains, FX, micro yields, micro crypto) with correct micro/standard tiers — and any rate that's still an estimate is clearly marked with an asterisk.

Reports now show one number per label: the preview KPIs match the exported report exactly (net of costs, commission-adjusted profit factor), and the 'compare to prior period' window is timezone-safe and snaps month scopes to the previous calendar month.
Analytics gains a 'Performance by tag' breakdown with an untagged-coverage row; calendar day tags surface in tooltips and drill-ins, and tag inputs autocomplete from your existing vocabulary.
The app loads faster: screens beyond the Dashboard now load on demand.

releases.title: A redesigned Blotterbook — new sidebar app on every screen
releases.summary: The whole app moves to a new sidebar layout with dedicated screens for your Dashboard, Calendar, Analytics, Blotter, CSV Library, Trade Editor, and Reports. Everything you could do before is still here — and a few things got better. As always, your trade data stays entirely in your browser.
highlights: New app shell: a persistent left sidebar (collapsible on desktop, a slide-over on mobile) replaces the stacked-panel layout, with each area now its own focused screen.
Dashboard modules: the Performance curve, Trading Calendar, Break-even & Cost, and Advanced Statistics are reorderable, hideable cards you can arrange — and save as named layouts to switch between.
Editable cost setup right on the dashboard: pick your broker, data feed, state, and platform fee in the Break-even & Cost card and watch commissions, tax, and take-home update live.
First-run onboarding: a new, empty app now walks you through setting up your cost model and importing your first CSV.
Saved filter views: name and reuse your favorite filter combinations. Plus per-trade screenshots, day-note tags, and one-click data backup / restore / erase.
Same private, local-only engine underneath — the redesign is a new coat of paint on the exact calculation core, now shipped to the main app and the demo together.

releases.title: Trade Blotter, in-context help, and more accurate cost estimates
releases.summary: The Trade Blotter graduates from the staging sandbox to the main app and demo, every metric now explains itself right where it lives, and the cost model gets more honest about subscriptions and unfamiliar contracts. As always, your trade data stays local to your browser.
highlights: Trade Blotter module: a full-width, scrollable table of every trade — date, symbol, side, quantity, modeled commission, and P&L — with an inline Note you can edit per trade. It tracks your active filters and scope like every other panel, and sits just below the Trading Calendar by default.

Definitions, in context: each metric's definition and caveats now ride in the panel or drill-down that owns it (Advanced Statistics, the stat-card pop-ups, the cost panel, the curve, the calendar), so the explanation is where you need it. The standalone Definitions panel is trimmed to the cross-cutting parsing caveats.

Truer subscription costs: fixed platform + data-feed fees are now counted for every calendar month from your first to your last trade — including months you didn't trade — since those fees bill whether or not you're active.

Unknown-contract warning: if an import contains a futures contract we don't yet have a contract size for, its P&L is flagged as estimated (instead of silently mis-priced) so you know to double-check it.
Behind the scenes: a correctness pass over the calculation engine tightened how distinct same-second trades and multi-lot exits are handled.

releases.title: Dashboard upgrades graduate from the staging sandbox
releases.summary: A batch of dashboard improvements move from the staging proving-ground to the main app and demo: a per-module header menu, a paginated trades table, and an account-level cost budget. As always, your trade data stays local to your browser.
highlights: Module menu: click the ⋯ on any dashboard panel header to collapse, reorder, or hide it — and re-add hidden modules from the new “+ Add module” menu.
Faster Manage-data table: large imports now page through 50 trades at a time instead of rendering thousands of rows at once, while search still spans your full history.
Account-level cost budget: the Break-even & Cost panel now reads as a stable all-time budget, independent of the scope toggle and filters (every other panel stays filter-driven).
Polish: the performance-report export is now usable on phone-width screens, and the redundant Changelog link was removed from the app top bar.

releases.title: The public site, rebuilt on the same modern foundation
releases.summary: The homepage, How-To, Roadmap, Changelog, and Legal pages now run on the same modern UI framework as the app — prerendered to static HTML so they stay fast and search-engine friendly. Nothing changes in what you see; this unifies the codebase behind the scenes and clears the way for richer pages ahead.
highlights: One foundation everywhere: the marketing and info pages share the app's component model, with the nav and footer now a single source instead of duplicated markup.
Still fast, still private: every page is server-rendered at build time for instant first paint and SEO, then enhanced in the browser — and, as always, no trade data ever leaves your browser.
No action needed: every page looks and works exactly as before; links and bookmarks are unchanged.

releases.title: Rebuilt under the hood, with tighter security
releases.summary: The app is now built on a modern UI framework across every surface — the main app, the demo, and the staging sandbox all run the same code. Nothing changes in how it looks or works; this clears the way for the richer charting and layout features on the roadmap and comes with a stronger security policy.
highlights: Same app, modern foundation: a single, shared codebase now powers the main app, the demo, and staging — fewer moving parts and a faster path to new features.
Tighter security: the page's content-security policy no longer allows inline styles anywhere, narrowing the surface for injection attacks. As always, your trade data never leaves the browser.
No action needed: your saved trades, notes, tags, screenshots, and layouts are untouched — everything works exactly as before.

releases.title: Faster, more accessible, more solid under the hood
releases.summary: A foundation pass: the app now loads as modern, self-contained modules — nothing changes in how it looks or works — alongside a batch of accessibility, performance, and reliability fixes.
highlights: Accessibility: the performance graph now reads each day's values aloud to screen readers as you arrow across it.
Performance: the performance graph stays snappy even with lots of day-notes saved.
Reliability: steadier trade imports and backup restores, and the data, export, and stat-detail dialogs now always release the page scroll when you close them.

releases.title: Tap a stat for the full story
releases.summary: The five headline stat cards on your dashboard are now clickable — open any of them for a focused breakdown with its own mini-chart. Promoted from the staging sandbox to the main app and the demo.
highlights: Click or tab+Enter on Net PnL, Win Rate, Profit Factor, Avg Win/Loss, or Max Drawdown to open a detail modal.
Each modal shows a metric-specific breakdown plus a focused inline chart (equity curve with peak→trough marks, day-of-week and per-symbol breakdowns, PnL distribution, and more).
Read-only — it's a deeper look at numbers you already have; nothing to configure or save.

releases.title: Filters refine the whole dashboard
releases.summary: Filtering once again narrows your entire dashboard — the calendar, stat cards, cost budget, statistics, and the performance graph all respond together, the way they always have.

releases.title: The full dashboard, everywhere
releases.summary: Every feature that was being trialed in the staging sandbox is now live on the main app and the demo — the journal you use day-to-day gets the whole redesigned dashboard.
highlights: New edge-to-edge web-grid dashboard on wide screens, with drag-to-reorder modules and a full-width performance graph.
Activity terminal, a session-status pill, and saveable workspace layouts.
Saved filter views, an in-app export-report modal (PDF / PNG / JPEG / Markdown + email), collapsible per-symbol cost & tax breakdowns, and day-note dots on the equity curve.

releases.title: Demo matches the app
releases.summary: The demo is now a faithful 1:1 mirror of the real app: every panel and control is present, just with data-changing actions disabled on the sample data.
highlights: Restored the Changelog link and the Day-notes section in the demo.
Data-mutating controls (load, import, backup, edit, delete) are clearly disabled rather than missing.

releases.title: Versioned release notes
releases.summary: This changelog is new. Instead of a raw list of code changes, it's now curated release notes — what actually shipped in each version, written to be read. You're looking at the first one.
highlights: Every release gets a plain-language summary and highlights, newest first.
Tracks the live production app and demo.

releases.title: Stability & security pass
releases.summary: A sweep of fixes from an internal audit — tightening up the calendar, your data, and the behind-the-scenes release machinery.
highlights: Clicking inside the data-manager or export popup no longer clears the calendar day you had selected.
Your journal note is saved the instant you switch days, closing a rare window where a quick edit could be dropped.
Imported backup screenshots are now validated before they're shown, and release versioning is hardened against edge cases.

releases.title: Admin & launch reliability
releases.summary: A maintenance release fixing the internal admin tools.
highlights: The admin version panel no longer hangs on load, and launching the staging environment is more reliable.

releases.title: Calendar notes & cost polish
releases.summary: Quality-of-life improvements to day-by-day journaling and the cost estimates that power your P&L.
highlights: Smoother editing of per-day notes straight from the calendar.
Refinements to commission, fee, and terminal-cost calculations.

releases.title: Automated release versioning
releases.summary: Every release now carries a real version number, derived automatically when changes ship — so this changelog and the in-app badge always agree on what you're running.
highlights: Version badge in the app header reflects the live release.
Separate prod and staging tracks behind the scenes.

releases.title: Beta released
releases.summary: The first public Beta of Blotterbook — a fast, private, browser-based futures-trading journal. Everything before automated versioning is rolled up here: the dashboard and equity curve, the demo, the marketing site, broker/platform format auto-detection, a CSV importer, an IndexedDB-backed data manager, filters, the day journal, and report export.
